Faster, more reliable lead delivery empowers sales teams to engage qualified
prospects sooner, boosting conversion rates.
800.448.0183 hello@informativ.com informativ.com

chunks.filename: Informativ Integrations - START HERE for Compliance.docx
chunks.content:
Informativ Compliance Integration Talk Tracks (Sales Reference — Start Here)

Key Message: "Informativ integrates to support the dealership workflow—not to force dealers to change it."

Each Compliance integration eliminates re-keying, keeps deals compliant, and helps dealers move faster without changing how they work. Lead with outcomes, not vendors or technical details. Only reference specific vendors if asked.

Compliance → CRM (AFDXML-Supported CRMs):
"Informativ pushes scanned DL lead into any CRM that supports AFDXML, so sales and managers always know where their customer stands without chasing paperwork."
Shortcut: "If the CRM speaks the industry standard, we're already there."

VIN Solutions (CRM) Integration:
"Informativ integrates with VIN Solutions to check for duplicate customer records in real time, keeping the CRM clean and preventing duplicate deals."

Compliance → LMS (Lender Management Systems — DealerTrack, RouteOne, CUDL):
"Informativ sends complete, compliant credit applications directly to lender systems so dealers can submit deals faster and avoid rework or funding delays."

Compliance → DMS:
"Informativ syncs completed compliance and credit documentation into the DMS to eliminate re-keying and keep every deal audit-ready."

Digital Retail → Compliance (Roadster, AutoFi, Gubagoo, Upstart):
"Digital Retail captures online buyer activity and automatically creates the Compliance DDJ to continue the online customer journey in the showroom."

Inventory → Compliance:
"Inventory providers send up-to-date vehicle details into Informativ so the correct vehicle information automatically appears on showroom floor forms."

If asked for vendor-specific detail, say: "If you'd like, I can verify the detailed list to ensure your vendor is supported and how the integration works." Product team can always help with technical validation calls, IT or compliance reviews, post-demo follow-up, and procurement or implementation discussions.

Informativ COMPLIANCE Integrations — Vendor-Level Detail (Last revised: Jan 26, 2026)

CRM Integrations:
- Any CRM supporting ADFXML: Informativ → CRM. Any CRM which supports ADFXML can receive leads from DL Scan in COMPLIANCE.
- VIN Solutions API: Informativ → CRM. Real-time duplicate check from VIN Solutions to reduce duplicate creation with DL Scan.
- Tekion CRM: CRM → Informativ. Download Tekion Leads into COMPLIANCE, prevent duplicates with DL Scan.
- DriveCentric: Informativ → CRM. Lead sent to CRM with DL Scan in COMPLIANCE via Secure FTP.

Lender Management Systems (LMS):
- DealerTrack: Informativ → DT. COMPLIANCE credit app push to DealerTrack.
- Route One: Informativ → Rt1. COMPLIANCE credit app push to RouteOne.
- CUDL: Informativ → CUDL. COMPLIANCE credit app push to CUDL.

DMS Integrations:
- Export DDJ to any DMS that supports importing PDF files.
- Tekion DMS: Enter Sold Tekion Deal ID in Informativ DDJ — sends documents to Tekion.

Digital Retail Integrations:
- Roadster → Informativ: Send Leads to COMPLIANCE, create DDJ.
- AutoFi → Informativ: Send Leads to COMPLIANCE, create DDJ.
- Gubagoo → Informativ: Send Leads to COMPLIANCE, create DDJ.
- Upstart → Informativ: Send Leads to COMPLIANCE, create DDJ.
- CarNow: Informativ → CarNow. Order Informativ Soft Pull from CarNow platform.

Inventory Integrations (all via flat file → Informativ):
Supported inventory providers: Vauto, Homenet, Dominion, Dealeron, DealerSocket, DealersLink, Max Digital, Inventory 43, Red Line, NextSteppe. All send inventory data to COMPLIANCE.

Product Roadmap (Target H2 2026):
- eLeads API: Informativ → CRM. Real-time duplicate check from eLeads, reduce duplicate creation.
- DealerSocket CRM API: Informativ → CRM. Real-time duplicate check from DealerSocket CRM.
- CDK Global DMS: Send Sold DDJ to CDK DMS. Not committed. 42% market share franchise dealers.
- COX Automotive (Dealertrack DMS): Send Sold DDJ to Dealertrack DMS. Not committed. 19% market share franchise dealers.
- Reynolds DMS: As of Q1 2025, Reynolds does not provide an API nor have plans to. 700Credit claims internal API access only. 16% market share franchise dealers.
- Solera (Auto/Mate DMS): Send Sold DDJ to Auto/Mate DMS. Not committed. 8% market share franchise dealers.

chunks.filename: Informativ Integrations - START HERE for Credit.docx
chunks.content:
Informativ Credit Integration Talk Tracks (Sales Reference — Start Here)

Core Message: "Informativ Credit integrates to help dealers move faster, spend smarter on credit, and keep every deal connected end-to-end."

Each Credit integration moves buyers through pre-qualification faster, reduces manual entry and duplicate work, and keeps credit activity compliant and connected to the deal. Lead with outcomes, not vendors.

Credit → CRM (AFDXML-Supported CRMs):
"Informativ sends credit leads into any AFDXML-supported CRM so sales teams can work deals without re-entering data."
Shortcut: "Credit leads show up where sales already lives."

CRM → Credit (Embedded Credit Ordering — DealerSocket):
"Sales can order Informativ credit products directly from DealerSocket CRM, keeping the deal moving without leaving their workflow."
Shortcut: "Pull credit without leaving DealerSocket CRM."

IMPORTANT SALES POSITIONING — Informativ intentionally limits embedded CRM credit ordering and instead invests in Protect, Enrich, Elevate, and Control — capabilities that differentiate our Compliance platform as the industry's only end-to-end, proactive showroom compliance enforcement solution that guarantees consistent compliant behavior. How to say it: "Rather than chasing CRM-specific credit buttons, Informativ focuses on enforcing compliance and protecting the dealer across the entire showroom workflow—where the real risk and value exist." Dealer model: CRM credit ordering = convenience. Informativ Compliance = control, protection, and differentiation. "Anyone can embed a credit button. Only Informativ enforces compliant behavior end to end."

Credit → LMS (DealerTrack, RouteOne):
"Informativ Credit sends complete credit applications to lender systems, helping dealers submit cleaner deals faster."
Shortcut: "Clean apps in, faster decisions out."

Credit → DMS:
"Informativ pushes pre-qualification and soft-pull links into the DMS so credit activity stays tied to the vehicle and the deal."
Shortcut: "Credit follows the deal into the DMS."

Digital Retail → Credit:
"Digital retail partners can trigger Informativ soft pulls so online buyers move forward before they ever hit the showroom."
Shortcut: "Online buyers arrive pre-qualified."

Affiliate Platforms → Credit (Credit API):
"Affiliate platforms can order Informativ credit products through our Credit API, allowing partners to embed credit without building it themselves."
Shortcut: "Partners sell credit without rebuilding credit."

Loan Origination Systems (LOS) → Credit:
"LOS platforms integrate with Informativ to run credit inside their funding workflow, reducing friction from application to approval."
Shortcut: "Credit runs inside the funding process."

If asked for vendor-specific detail, say: "If you'd like, I can verify the detailed list to ensure your vendor is supported and how the integration works." Product team can help with technical validation, IT reviews, post-demo follow-up, and procurement discussions.

Informativ CREDIT Integrations — Vendor-Level Detail (Last revised: Jan 26, 2026)

CRM Integrations:
- Any CRM supporting ADFXML: Informativ → CRM. Send lead from CREDIT Web App.
- DealerSocket CRM: CRM → Informativ. SocketCredit (exclusive to CREDIT) — order Informativ credit products from within DealerSocket.
- Momentum CRM: Informativ → CRM. Lead sent from Informativ Credit Web App.
- ABCoA (cyclCRM): Informativ → CRM. Lead sent from Informativ Credit Web App.

Lender Management Systems (LMS):
- DealerTrack: Bidirectional. Informativ credit app push to DealerTrack. Informativ credit products orderable from within DealerTrack.
- Route One: Bidirectional. Informativ credit app push to RouteOne. Informativ credit products orderable from within RouteOne.

DMS Integrations:
- Advent: Informativ → DMS. Prequal + SP link push.
- IDMS DealerTrack: Informativ → DMS. Prequal + SP link push.
- PBS Systems: Informativ → DMS. Prequal + SP link push.
- Quorum (xsellerator): Informativ → DMS. Prequal + SP link push.
- ABCoA (DealPack): Informativ → DMS. Prequal + SP link push.

Digital Retail:
- CarNow → Informativ: Order Informativ Soft Pull from CarNow platform.
- Motive → Informativ: Order Informativ Soft Pull; after credit return, Lead + SP sent to COMPLIANCE.

Affiliate Platforms (Credit API — Order Informativ Credit Products from Affiliate Platform):
- iLending Direct → Informativ: Soft Pull ordering.
- Yield Solutions → Informativ: Soft Pull ordering.
- CPS Cars → Informativ: Soft Pull ordering.
- Finance of America → Informativ: Mismo Format — exclusively mortgages.
- AppOne → Informativ: Mismo Format — focused on Recreational Vehicle dealers and groups.
- Micro21 → Informativ: Credco Rev Share — CRM.
- Blue Water Finance → Informativ: Credco (Bluenet) end user integration customer.
- Northteq/Tamarack → Informativ: LOS (Loan Origination System) integration.
- Seafunds → Informativ: LOS integration.
- Leasepoint → Informativ: LOS integration.
- Taycor Financial → Informativ: Integrated with CREDIT products.
- Customized Dealer Fin Svcs (CDFS) → Informativ: CRM integration.

Informativ Technology Compatibility Matrix — Dealer Technology Stack Reference

When discussing integrations with prospects, Informativ reps should understand the major dealer technology platforms across four categories: CRM, DMS, LOS, and Credit Provider. This reference helps reps position Informativ's integration capabilities during sales conversations.

CRM Platforms in the Dealer Market:
VinSolutions (Cox Automotive ecosystem, largest franchise CRM footprint), Elead CRM (CDK Global ecosystem, strong enterprise/large group adoption), DealerSocket (strong desking tools with built-in marketing automation), DriveCentric (2,200+ dealers, modern AI-powered CRM gaining share), ProMax (all-in-one platform with CRM, desking, and compliance), AutoRaptor (independent dealer focused, lower price point), Tekion CRM (cloud-native, unified CRM/DMS, growing OEM partnerships), PBS Systems (integrated CRM/DMS, mid-market), Fullpath (AI-powered CDP with CRM capabilities, marketing-led), CDK CRM (bundled with CDK DMS, equity mining and AI tools).

DMS Platforms:
CDK Global (~40% franchise market share, anchor DMS for large groups), Reynolds & Reynolds (~35% franchise share, closed ecosystem with strong F&I integration), Tekion (cloud-native disruptor, unified CRM/DMS, growing OEM endorsements), Dealertrack DMS (Cox Automotive ecosystem, strong LOS/DMS bundle), DealerBuilt (open-architecture DMS, independent dealer groups), Autosoft (cloud-based DMS, mid-market), Auto/Mate now under Solera/DealerSocket (value-tier DMS), PBS Systems (integrated CRM/DMS, Canadian and US mid-market), Quorum (niche DMS, select dealer groups), Dominion VUE (Dominion Dealer Solutions, integrated platform).

LOS Platforms (effectively a three-platform market):
DealerTrack (largest lender-dealer network, 22,000+ dealers, Cox ecosystem), RouteOne (neutral open platform, strong digital contracting/e-vaulting — RouteOne's credit bureau page lists Informativ as authorized credit provider), CUDL/Origence (credit union lending network, 1,100 CUs, 20,000 dealers — additive LOS typically used alongside DealerTrack or RouteOne, rebranding to Origence).

Credit Provider Competitive Landscape:
Informativ (our platform — credit, compliance, fraud detection, pre-qual, and deal workflow in one solution), 700Credit (largest competitor, 21,000+ dealers, broad compliance/ID verification suite — primary displacement target), NCC/National Credit Center (credit & compliance solutions for auto dealers — secondary displacement target), Agility Credit (smaller competitor, regional presence — lower-tier displacement opportunity).

Sales Application: When discussing technology with a prospect, understanding their tech stack (CRM + DMS + LOS + Credit Provider) reveals integration compatibility, competitive displacement opportunity, and the right positioning for Informativ's value proposition. Dealers on platforms where Informativ has live integrations experience lower implementation friction and faster time-to-value.

chunks.title: RouteOne Integration — Product One-Pager
chunks.content:
ROUTEONE INTEGRATION — Streamlined Compliance & Deal Submission with Digital Deal Jackets

Informativ's integration with RouteOne simplifies and accelerates your data transfer process and allows for instant updates whenever there are changes to an application — keeping data current and accurate. The integration ensures the sales process remains compliant, reducing risk and providing peace of mind.

HOW IT WORKS — Deal Jacket Integration:
To submit directly to RouteOne from Informativ's Compliance Center (where all info needed for the sale is collected, created, and stored):
1. Click on the customer's name
2. Find "Customer Actions"

This action pushes the application to RouteOne's Dashboard. The most recent credit app is always listed at the top.

WHY IT MATTERS FOR SALES POSITIONING:

- Combined with Verified STIPS, this gives a closed-loop "lead → compliance → verified stips → RouteOne → funded" workflow

pricing.label: Pricing & Packaging
pricing.description: Tiered packages (Protect/Enrich/Elevate/Control), ROI calculator, pricing strategy

chunks.content:
Informativ Pricing Governance — Effective March 2026

Pricing Approval Thresholds by Package Tier

Each package tier has its own Target GM%, Manager Approval Threshold, and Deal Desk Floor. No deal should be priced below the tier's Deal Desk Floor.

Package Tier | Target GM% | Manager Approval Threshold | Deal Desk Floor (Minimum)
Protect      | 47.5%      | 42.5%                      | 39.5%
Enrich       | 52.5%      | 47.5%                      | 44.5%
Elevate      | 60.0%      | 55.0%                      | 52.0%
Control      | 50.0%      | 45.0%                      | 42.0%

How Approval Works:
- At or above Target GM%: Standard pricing, no approval needed.
- Between Target and Manager Threshold: Manager approval required.
- Below Manager Threshold: Deal Desk review required. The Deal Desk will optimize pricing upward. No deal will be priced below the tier-specific floor.

The Deal Desk Floor for each tier is set at 3 percentage points below the Manager Approval Threshold. This replaces the prior flat 39% floor that applied across all tiers.

Key Principle: Higher-value packages (Elevate, Control) carry higher margin expectations. Reps should anchor pricing conversations on value delivered, not cost. Use the Informativ Method: teach the dealer what they are losing today, tailor the package to their business model, and take control of the pricing conversation by framing margin protection as a benefit to the dealer (better support, faster implementation, dedicated resources).

Informativ Monetization Full Documentation
May 2025

01 Introduction
Project goals: To develop an optimal monetization strategy for Informativ we need to
accomplish several objectives
1. Understand competitive landscape and price “elasticity”.
Informativ would like to develop a
 Identify needs of Informativ’s customers and prospects, assess market dynamics and
monetization strategy that helps
uncover pain-points with current offering for franchised dealerships and independents.
Informativ to increase revenue and
 Understand purchasing process and CSF for decision makers/ key stakeholders of
profitability by $5M to $10M
franchised dealerships and independents and identify source and size of budgets.
 Assess Informativ’s perceived value position against key competitors (e.g. 700credit,
NCC) and opportunity to take price and/or expand share of wallet within existing customer.
2. Develop ideal packaged offering that drives product adoption and upsell.
 Identify value drivers, features and benefits that are most valuable to franchised dealers
and independents and that drive adoption and perceived relative performance.
 Create offering for franchised dealerships that maximizes up-sellingof new features and
helps to expand share of wallet.
 Create reduced offering for independentsthat supports market expansion while limiting
down-sell risks.
3. Develop ideal pricing strategy for product offering to increase revenue and
profitability.
 Identify price model and metrics that best align with customers’ perceived value to drive
revenue and helps to increase the penetration of the subscription offering vs transactional.
 Measurewillingness-to-pay and set optimal price levels to drive customer acquisition and
volume, increase share of wallet (with franchised dealers), while increasing profitability.
 Develop plan for future year price increases, including list of activities and triggers.

02 Project Approach
Approach details - Competitive positioning: We will identify key value drivers to evaluate
against competitors during market research

# Value drivers Description
1 Brand reputation Public perception of firm’s trust, quality of offering, and reliability.
2 Breadth of features Variety and scope of functionalities offered.
3 Customizability Ability to configure and outputs to specific needs.
4 Ease of use Simplicity with which users can navigate and use the solution without extensive training.
6 Customer support Convenience and effectiveness of interactions incl. purchasing, onboarding, and support.
7 Ease of implementation Ease and time to implement solution, integrate into existing systems, and generate business value.
8 Innovation & feature roadmap Extent to which new features and improvements are added to theplatform (e.g. AI functionalities)
9 Integrations Ability to integrate seamlessly with current tech stack (e.g., CRM, DMS, etc.)
10 Price Total cost of purchasing a solution including initial/setup fees.
11 Scalability Ability of solution to grow and adapt as the organization’s needs and volume expand.
12 Security & compliance Certainty that software not only protects sensitive data but also adheres to legal and regulatory standards.
13 Fraud reduction Ability of offering to reduce instances of fraudulent purchase.
14 Compliance improvement Ability of offering to enforce compliance rules, mitigating lawsuit risk from data storage or unethical behavior.
15 Data retention Ease of finding deal jackets and all included items such as customer STIPs, etc.
16 … …

the market that we should test?

XS
Executive Summary
Project objectives and overview:
 Simon-Kucher engaged with Informativto optimize pricing strategy. Key focus areas were to clarify market perception of the competitive landscape,
and to develop an optimized packaging and pricing structure to drive product adoption and improve revenue and profitability.
 Goal of $5-10M profit and revenue improvement identified.
Project approach
 Pricing optimization was developed through internal and external market research -interviews and survey with the Informativteam, data analysis, and
primary market research with customer and prospect interviews and survey. This was in conjunction with SK benchmarks and bestpractices.
Improvement levers
 Improve packaging structure to focus on margin and target down-market expansion: Transition to good-better-best (GBB) structure enables
customers to select the right package for their willingness-to-pay (WTP) and usage requirements while protecting margin via reduced discounting.
 Monetize aberrant customer usage behavior: Enact policy that monetizes overage for customers aligning price to value received and reducing
gamesmanship thereby reducing margin compression risk through non-monetized COGS.
 Transition from negotiated discount to earned discount: Enable sales teams to ask for tradeoff in negotiations changing conversation from pure price
discussion to value oriented understanding of what items can be removed to reach WTP price points.
 Enable usage commitments and credit rollover to drive loyalty: Deploy commitment level to gain buy-in via earned discount from customers. In the
event of end-of-term unused credits, allow rollover to capitalize on sunk-cost mentality of customers and minimize churn of transactional customers.
Financial impact & roadmap
 Overall, the potential revenue uplift across the identifiedopportunitiesis $5M (+6%) in Y1 and $18M (+21%) in Y3 compared to status quo, with $12M
(+32%) profit uplift in Y3.
 Immediate improvements can start to hit financials in late CY25 with adoption of price model for new customers.
Source: Simon-Kucher
Simon-Kucher | Informativ | Monetization Strategy | May 2025 240

threshold. This leads to:

 Sales tend tend to offer the maximum discount allowed without triggering approval
 While tiered discounting levels (Tier 1/2/3) exist, they are not being used as a strategic
lever—discounting behavior is driven more by sales discretion than deal characteristics

03 Recommendations
Overview Adhere to psychological thresholds
Observations and best practices
Informativ’scurrent credit pricing is aligned with competitors and generally perceived as solid in
the market. Interviews and internal findings reveal that:
 Price is not the primary purchase driver for customers: Key functionalities (e.g., ease of use,
fraud protection, ease of implementation)rank higher
 Customers typically don’t recall prices after contracts are signed, presenting opportunity of
incremental increases
 Best practices in digital pricing suggest optimizing price points to align with psychological
thresholds (e.g., $0.99, $9.49) and using rounding anchors to drive price acceptance
Benefits to Informativ Recommended actions
Incremental margin lift: Optimized pricing Adjust credit list prices to align with
drives direct bottom-line impact psychological price points
Low risk of churn or resistance: Track customer feedback after renewals
Customers pay little attention to the exact to validate price tolerance
price after signing, and current pricing
remains aligned with competitors
Psychological thresholds can be used to
fine-tune pricing without requiring major
structural changes
Source: Simon-Kucher

03 Recommendations
Overview Introduce annual price escalators
Observations and best practices
Informativ’scurrent contracts do not systematically include annual price increases, which limits
the ability to capture growing product value or adjust for inflation:
 Price sensitivity is low for existing customersduring small, predictable actions, especially
when clearly communicated in advance. Many customers already budget for this
 It's common practice to build annual escalators (e.g., 3–5%) to normalize expectations for
yearly adjustments and reduce negotiation friction at renewal
Benefits to Informativ Recommended actions
Margin protection: Contractual price Include standard annual price escalators
escalators help offset rising costs and (e.g., 3–5%) in all new contracts and
inflation without needing to renegotiate renewal templates
each year
Communicate escalators as industry-
Lower renewal friction: Pre-agreed standard practice, tied to ongoing product
increases reduce pricing shocks and development and cost of service
remove difficult pricing conversations increases
from the renewal cycle

chunks.title: How to Position Which Tier (Decision Tree)
chunks.content:
TIER POSITIONING DECISION TREE — Use this when a dealer asks "Which tier fits us?"

Q1: Does the dealer currently use ANY compliance automation or fraud prevention beyond what their CRM ships with?

  NO → Position PROTECT.
    Reasoning: They're new to compliance. Protect is the entry tier and delivers the biggest compliance + fraud value with the smallest behavior change. SmartPencil and soft pulls would be a bigger lift than they're ready for.

  YES → Continue to Q2.

Q2: Does the dealer currently leverage soft pulls in their sales process? (Pre-qualification, lead-stage credit, etc.)

  NO → Position ENRICH (next step from Protect or from Compliance-Only legacy product).
    Reasoning: SmartPencil layered on top of compliance gives them desk efficiency without the soft-pull learning curve. They get a faster first-pencil, better PVR, and the same compliance enforcement.

  YES → Continue to Q3.

Q3: Is the dealer's HARD pull volume per delivered car HIGH (>5) or LOW (<3)?

  HIGH → Position CONTROL.
    Reasoning: Bundled hard pulls at flat rate are more economical at high volume. Control also gives them credit guardrails for both hard AND soft pulls, plus complete visibility.

  LOW → Position ELEVATE.
    Reasoning: Transactional hard pulls keep credit spend low. Soft-pull-first workflows handle the bulk of credit decisioning. They get spend controls to keep hard pulls disciplined.

  GRAY ZONE (3-5 hard pulls per delivered car) → Run both scenarios in the Internal Pricing Calculator and present the lower-cost option. Use credit spend trajectory (growing or shrinking?) as the tiebreaker.

ANTI-PATTERNS (do not do):
- Do NOT downsell an existing Total Solution / Control customer to Enrich without strong cost justification. They will lose visibility into the soft-pull score/report and feel they've been demoted.
- Do NOT position Foundation or Detect — those tiers were retired October 2025 and do not exist in the current product set.
- Do NOT quote internal pricing details to the customer. Use the External Price Sheet generated by the Pricing Calculator.

Conversation starters for the discovery call (per persona):
- CEO/Dealer Principal: "How do you ensure compliance is enforced across all rooftops? What happens when a deal gets recontracted?"
- CFO: "What is your current credit report spend across rooftops? Do you have monthly variance in F&I funding due to recontracts? What does one fraud loss cost you?"
- GSM: "How do you know reps are following your process? How often are deals delayed in F&I?"
- F&I Director: "What's your funding cycle time today? How often do STIPs delay funding?"
- Compliance Officer: "How are you handling Red Flags today? When was your last audit and what did they flag?"

chunks.source: Dealer Payment Glossary for Finance & Leasing Deals
chunks.filename: Dealer Payment Glossary for Finance & Leasing Deals.docx
chunks.content:
## Dealer Payment Glossary for Finance & Leasing Deals



### 1. MSRP (Manufacturer’s Suggested Retail Price)

Definition: The retail price recommended by the manufacturer for a new vehicle.
Purpose: Used for lease residual calculations, incentive amounts, and MSRP-based rebates.
Displayed On: The Monroney window sticker.


### 2. Sales Price (Selling Price)

Definition: The final negotiated price of the vehicle before taxes and fees.
Impact: Determines the loan amount for financing or the capitalized cost for leasing.
Adjusted By: Rebates, trade-ins, dealer discounts, or market adjustments.


### 3. Invoice Price

Definition: The price billed to the dealer by the manufacturer.
Note: Often higher than true cost due to holdback or factory-to-dealer incentives.
Use: Establishes baseline for front-end profit.


### 4. Bookout Value

Definition: The market or lender-recognized value of a vehicle, used for loan approval and LTV calculations.
Sources: NADA, KBB, J.D. Power, or Black Book.
Impact: Lenders limit advances based on this number.


### 5. Odometer

Definition: The mileage reading of a vehicle.
Impact:
High mileage reduces book value and max advance.
Lenders often cap mileage (e.g., 120k–150k miles).
Compliance: Accurate odometer disclosure required by law.


### 6. Buyer’s ZIP Code

Definition: Determines tax, title, registration, local incentives, and regional lender programs.
Impact:
Directly affects payment due to location-based tax laws.
Lenders and OEMs offer different rates and programs by ZIP region.
System Integration: DMS/F&I software automatically calculates based on ZIP.


### 7. Money Factor (MF)

Definition: The decimal representation of a lease’s interest rate.
Conversion: Multiply by 2,400 to approximate APR.
Example: 0.00125 × 2400 = 3.0% APR.
Impact: Lower MF = lower lease payment.


### 8. Interest Rate (APR)

Definition: The annual percentage rate charged by a lender for borrowing money.
Used For: Retail finance (loans).
Lease Equivalent: Money Factor (MF).


### 9. Credit Score

Definition: A numeric value (300–850) representing a consumer’s creditworthiness.
Used For: Lender tier assignment, interest rate, and approval level.
Typical Tiers:
Tier 1: 740+
Tier 2: 700–739
Tier 3: 650–699
Tier 4: <650
Caveat: Each lender applies its own risk model; captives also use Risk Scorecards.


### 10. Risk Scorecard

Definition: A lender’s proprietary risk model combining credit bureau data with loan structure and collateral.
Inputs: Credit score, DTI, PTI, LTV, and term.
Impact: Determines buy rate, tier, and stipulations.
Dealer Tip: Explains why customers with identical credit scores receive different approvals from different lenders.


### 11. Residual Value

Definition: The estimated value of a leased vehicle at the end of its term.
Expressed As: A percentage of MSRP (e.g., 58%).
Set By: The lessor, not negotiable.
Impact: Higher residual = lower lease payment.


### 12. Capitalized Cost (Cap Cost)

Definition: The total amount being financed in a lease.
Includes: Vehicle price, acquisition fee, optional add-ons, and rolled-in charges.


### 13. Capitalized Cost Reduction

Definition: Any upfront payment or credit applied to lower the total cap cost.
Examples: Cash down payment, trade equity, or manufacturer rebates.


### 14. Down Payment

Definition: The buyer’s initial cash contribution toward the total amount due.
Includes: Typically covers tax, title, license (TTL), and dealer fees — unless those are financed.
Max Out-of-Pocket:
When a customer specifies a fixed total amount they can pay at signing (e.g., “$3,000 total out of pocket”).
The dealer structures all due-at-signing items (down, TTL, first payment) within that total; anything beyond is rolled into the finance or lease.
Impact: Reduces monthly payment and total interest owed.


### 15. Out-of-Pocket Costs

Definition: Total cash due at signing (loan or lease).
Includes: Down payment, first payment, TTL, doc, and acquisition fees.
Difference From Down Payment: Out-of-pocket is the total due, while the down payment is only the portion applied to vehicle cost.


### 16. Trade-In Actual Cash Value (ACV)

Definition: The dealer’s true wholesale value of the trade-in vehicle.


### 17. Trade Allowance

Definition: The value shown to the customer for their trade on the deal paperwork.
Note: May differ from ACV depending on deal presentation or structure.


### 18. Trade Payoff

Definition: The remaining balance owed on the customer’s trade-in vehicle loan.
Impact: Determines positive or negative equity applied to the new deal.


### 19. Rebates & Incentives

Definition: OEM or lender discounts used to lower sale price or payment.
Types:
Customer Cash – Direct discount to buyer.
Finance/Lease Support – Reduced APRs or MFs through captives.
Dealer Cash – Backend bonus to the dealership.
Regional: Varies by buyer ZIP or market.


### 20. Lender Programs

Definition: Structured rate and term offerings from lenders, including standard and incentivized programs.
Types:
Standard Rates: Regular APR/MF without OEM support.
Incentivized Rates: OEM-subsidized programs offering special APRs or reduced MFs (e.g., 0.9% APR or 36-month lease special).
Restrictions: Incentivized programs often can’t be combined with cash rebates.
Timing: Updated monthly with OEM program calendars.
Dealer Tip: Verify eligibility and stackability during each month’s program rollout.


### 21. Stairstep Programs

Definition: OEM-to-dealer volume incentive programs that pay bonuses when sales thresholds are met.
Mechanism:
Incentives increase incrementally as sales volumes hit preset levels (e.g., 50, 75, 100 units).
Evaluated monthly or quarterly.
Impact: Encourages high-volume sales; may drive aggressive end-of-month pricing.


### 22. Markup

Definition: Dealer-added profit from increasing a lender’s buy rate, MF, or product cost.
Compliance: Regulated and capped by lender policies.

chunks.source: Dealer Payment Glossary for Finance & Leasing Deals
chunks.filename: Dealer Payment Glossary for Finance & Leasing Deals.docx
chunks.content:
### 23. Rate Markup

Definition: The spread between a lender’s buy rate and the rate sold to the customer.
Creates: Dealer reserve income (back-end profit).
Limit: Typically 2.0% APR or 0.00040 MF.


### 24. Flat

Definition: Fixed payment from a lender for deals sold at or below buy rate (e.g., $200–$250).


### 25. Super Flat

Definition: Larger flat (e.g., $400–$800) paid by lender to encourage dealer volume or preferred program use.


### 26. Split

Definition: Profit-sharing agreement between dealer and lender, often for reserve or backend profit (e.g., 70/30).


### 27. Acquisition Fee

Definition: Lessor fee to originate and service a lease (typically $595–$1,095).


### 28. Disposition Fee

Definition: Fee charged at lease-end for vehicle return (typically $350–$595).


### 29. Depreciation

Definition: The loss in vehicle value over time.
Lease Formula: Cap Cost – Residual Value


### 30. Lease Term

Definition: Lease duration in months (usually 24–60).
Impact: Longer = lower monthly payment, higher overall cost.


### 31. Mileage Allowance

Definition: Annual mileage limit for a lease (typically 10k–15k miles).
Excess Miles: Charged per mile (e.g., $0.15–$0.30).


### 32. Buy Rate

Definition: The lender’s base interest rate or MF before markup.
Determines: Dealer’s potential reserve or flat pay.


### 33. Dealer Reserve

Definition: Dealer profit from rate or MF markup.
Cap: Typically 2% APR or 0.00040 MF.


### 34. LTV (Loan-to-Value Ratio)

Definition: The percentage of vehicle value being financed.
Formula: (Loan Amount ÷ Vehicle Value) × 100
Typical Limit: Up to 120% to include taxes, fees, and F&I products.


### 35. Max Advance

Definition: The maximum financing percentage a lender allows based on credit tier and collateral.
Example: 125% for Tier 1, 110% for Tier 3.


### 36. Loan or Finance Calculation

Definition: The method used to calculate a customer’s monthly loan payment.
Formula:
Payment=r(1+r)n(1+r)n−1×P\text{Payment} = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} \times PPayment=(1+r)n−1r(1+r)n ×P
Where:
PPP = principal (amount financed)
rrr = monthly interest rate (APR ÷ 12)
nnn = total months
Impact: Payment increases with higher rate or shorter term; decreases with more down payment or longer term.


### 37. Lease Calculation

Definition: The process of determining a monthly lease payment using depreciation and finance charges.
Formula:
Payment=Depreciation Charge+Finance Charge\text{Payment} = \text{Depreciation Charge} + \text{Finance Charge}Payment=Depreciation Charge+Finance Charge
Depreciation Charge: (Cap Cost−Residual)÷Term(\text{Cap Cost} - \text{Residual}) ÷ \text{Term}(Cap Cost−Residual)÷Term
Finance Charge: (Cap Cost+Residual)×Money Factor(\text{Cap Cost} + \text{Residual}) × \text{Money Factor}(Cap Cost+Residual)×Money Factor
Includes: Taxes (varies by state), and possibly acquisition or add-on fees.
Impact: Higher residuals or lower money factors reduce payments.


### 38. Loan Payment

Definition: The fixed monthly payment on a retail finance contract.
Includes: Principal and interest.
Factors: Loan amount, APR, term, and down payment.
Formula Reference: Same as Loan or Finance Calculation.


### 39. Lease Payment

Definition: The total monthly payment on a lease contract.
Includes: Depreciation charge, rent charge (finance), taxes, and sometimes acquisition fee.
Influenced By: Cap cost, residual, money factor, and term.
Typically: Lower than a comparable loan payment because the lessee only pays for depreciation.


### 40. Tax

Definition: Sales or use tax on vehicle transactions.
Calculated By: Buyer’s ZIP; varies by state.


### 41. Title

Definition: Legal proof of vehicle ownership; lists lienholder until payoff.


### 42. License (Registration)

Definition: State-issued registration allowing vehicle operation on public roads.


### 43. Documentation (Doc) Fee

Definition: Dealer administrative fee for processing paperwork (usually $150–$799; regulated by state).


### 44. Payoff

Definition: The balance required to close out a loan or lease early.


### 45. Front-End Profit

Definition: Gross profit from the vehicle sale itself.
Formula: Selling Price – Invoice – Trade Adjustments.


### 46. Back-End Profit

Definition: Profit from F&I activities such as reserve and product sales.


### 47. F&I Menu

Definition: Structured, compliant presentation tool used to disclose and sell protection products.


### 48. F&I Products

Definition: Optional protection and service plans added to the sale or lease (e.g., Service Contract, GAP, Tire & Wheel, Key Replacement).


### 49. Captive Lender

Definition: Manufacturer-owned finance arm offering special programs to brand dealers (e.g., Ford Credit, Toyota Financial, GM Financial).


### 50. Credit Union

Definition: Member-owned lender offering competitive rates and personalized underwriting.


### 51. National Bank

Definition: Large federally chartered institution offering broad auto finance programs (e.g., Chase, Wells Fargo, Capital One).


### 52. DTI (Debt to Income)

Definition: A ratio used by lenders to measure a borrower’s ability to manage monthly payments and repay debts. Formula: (Total Monthly Debt ÷ Gross Monthly Income) × 100. A lower DTI ratio indicates stronger creditworthiness and repayment capacity.


### 53. PTI (Payment to Income)

Definition: A ratio used by lenders to evaluate how much of a borrower’s gross monthly income will go toward the vehicle payment. Formula: (Proposed Monthly Payment ÷ Gross Monthly Income) × 100. Lenders use PTI limits to ensure the borrower’s payment is affordable.


### 54. TTL (Tax, Title, License)

An abbreviation for the combined costs of tax, title, and license fees required to register and legally operate a vehicle. TTL varies by state and county and is typically included in the customer’s out-of-pocket or financed amount.
See section 40,41,42

Stairstep Programs | ❌ | ✅ | OEM
Loan & Lease Calculations | ✅ | ❌ | Dealer / Lender
LTV / Max Advance | ✅ | ✅ | Lender

Term / Mileage | ✅ | ❌ | Lender

Go-To-Market Structure / Organization
Please discuss how the sales function has evolved over the past several years, including changes in headcount by role and any structural shifts in coverage
Please provide an overview of the current sales organization, including responsibilities, focus areas, and reporting lines. Please discuss charts below
How are salespeople managed / distributed in terms of geographical coverage? Please discuss image below / how large existing the opportunity is by region
Please outline the standardized sales process across the full cycle from inbound or outbound contact through close, including typical timelines, key stages, and tools used to drive productivity
Please describe which sales practices have proven most effective and which approaches have been less effective, and please explain the underlying drivers
Please describe the most common win reasons and loss reasons in competitive deals, including the role of pricing, compliance capabilities, bureau cost dynamics, lender connectivity, workflow integration, and implementation burden
Sales Compensation and Incentives (Received “2.8 Sales Commission Plan Scheme for 2026”)
Please describe how sales performance is measured, including the KPIs used to manage the team and the cadence of performance review.
Please describe whether sales representatives are compensated on commissions, how commissions are structured, and what typical ramp time is before representatives become fully productive
Please describe historical quota attainment by salesperson
Tier Packaging, Upgrades, and Guardrails
Please walk us through the new tier pricing and packaging structure (Protect, Enrich, Elevate, and Control), including the ideal customer profile for each tier, the included feature set (including SmartPencil and hard pull / soft pull construct), and how the tier lineup changes the Company’s approach to upsell and cross-sell versus the legacy tier set
Describe the different target customers for each tier (ideal vs actual)
Please discuss what management learned from prior tier performance (including why Foundation and Protect did not gain traction and why buyers selected Protect), and what specific changes were made to create clearer value, pricing, and gross profit step-ups between tiers
How do guardrails factor into the sales motion (i.e., how do you message “guardrails” to customers to avoid escalations while protecting margin)?
How does the Company handle customer pushback tied to price increases / overages, and how does that influence upsell / cross-sell conversion?
Please describe the specific guardrails embedded in the new tier model (usage limits, triggers, enforcement), and how guardrails differ by tier
Pipeline, Conversion, and Forecasting
Please describe how opportunities enter the pipeline, how pipeline stages are defined, and whether standardized stage weights are used in forecasting
Please discuss current pipeline size and quality; to what degree have NADA-generated opportunities begun to convert?
Please quantify the current channel-sourced pipeline contribution and conversion expectations (including NADA channel partner pipeline), and describe how those opportunities differ in sales cycle and win rates relative to direct opportunities
Please discuss historical pipeline conversion rates and how the pipeline is updated and reviewed internally, including any standard dashboards used by management
Marketing and Demand Generation
Please describe how marketing initiatives are carried out and how sales efforts inform marketing strategy and vice versa
Please share one or two examples of successful marketing initiatives and describe any measurable outcomes or key metrics used to evaluate impact
Please describe the channel partner compensation structure (e.g., referral fees, commissions, rev share), including how partner economics differ by product
Packaging, Pricing, and ROI
Please walk through the pricing model and typical contract terms by product line and describe any pricing pressures in the market, and how does this vary for TS and Credit standalone?
Please provide an update on pricing changes, elimination of low-margin customers, increased guardrails
Please describe how Informativ demonstrates return on investment to prospective customers during sales conversations and whether return on investment is quantified in a consistent way
How does this differ for TS and Credit standalone?
Customer Journey / Account Success
Please walk through the customer journey from onboarding through renewal and describe how Informativ drives cross-sell and upsell within the existing base
Please describe the most common reasons for churn and the operational and commercial actions taken to prevent churn
How is market-driven upsell evaluated separately from true upsell?
Requested Follow-Ups
Please provide a current pipeline export and any historical pipeline and conversion reporting

chunks.source: Project Forge - GTM Sales Deep Dive Responses
chunks.filename: Project Forge - GTM Sales Deep Dive Responses.docx
chunks.content:
CONFIDENTIAL
INFORMATIV
Project Forge
GTM & Sales Deep Dive — Management Responses
Prepared by: Michael Byrd, Chief Revenue Officer
Date: February 2026
Classification: Confidential — For Due Diligence Use Only


# 1. Go-To-Market Structure / Organization



## 1.1 Sales Function Evolution

The Informativ sales organization has undergone meaningful structural evolution since the company transitioned from a legacy compliance/credit point-solution provider to a unified SaaS platform. The key inflection points are as follows:
2022–2023 (Integration Phase): Informativ operated with a fragmented product set — CBC (credit), DSGSS (compliance), and CreditDriver — each with separate sales motions. The team was smaller and largely reactive, with deal sourcing concentrated in inbound and prior-business-relationship channels. The Salesforce CRM implementation during this period established the foundation for pipeline visibility and rep accountability.
2023–2024 (Total Solution Launch): The company repackaged three disparate products into the unified Total Solution (TS), repositioning Informativ from a point-solution vendor to a platform-based compliance partner. This drove 30%+ annual bookings growth and enabled a shift from transactional to recurring SaaS revenue. Sales headcount expanded alongside dedicated Sales Development Representatives and Regional Sales Managers organized by geography.
2024–2026 (SaaS-First Tier Restructure): The tier architecture was redesigned from Protect (credit-first, low-margin entry tiers) to the current Protect/Enrich/Elevate/Control model — anchoring every customer on the compliance platform as the SaaS baseline. This structural change was supported by a Simon-Kucher pricing engagement and is designed to drive higher Gross Margin and Gross Profit dollars per customer relationship. SmartPencil (deal desking/payments) and Verified STIPs were added as high-margin product extensions.


## 1.2 Current Sales Organization

The sales function reports to the Chief Revenue Officer (Michael Byrd). The organization is structured across two distinct tracks: a direct sales team managed by two Directors of Sales, and a Business Development function led by a VP of Business Development overseeing affiliate and channel programs.
Business Development Program Definitions
Affiliate Program: Affiliates are companies that share a similar customer base to Informativ (e.g., automotive-adjacent service providers, F&I vendors, dealer technology companies). Affiliates either resell Informativ products directly to their customer base or refer deals to the direct sales team in exchange for a commission. The Affiliate Program Manager manages the existing affiliate portfolio and drives revenue through ongoing engagement, while the BD Manager — New Affiliates is responsible for identifying and contracting net new affiliate relationships.
Channel Partnership Program: Channel partnerships involve selling Informativ's Payments technology and API into upmarket solutions that serve automotive dealers. Target channel partners include dealership website providers, marketing technology platforms, automotive chatbot and digital retailing tools, and desking/F&I software vendors. These integrations embed Informativ's capabilities natively into the dealer's existing workflow, creating a distribution channel that extends reach beyond the direct sales force. The BD Manager — New Channel Partners is responsible for identifying, developing, and contracting these integrations.


## 1.3 Geographic Coverage

Sales coverage is organized by geographic territory across the continental U.S. RSMs carry defined regional responsibility with account assignments managed through Salesforce. Coverage strategy is calibrated to dealership density and franchise concentration, with heavier investment in the Southeast, Midwest, and major metro markets. The NADA annual conference serves as a primary national pipeline generation event, supplemented by regional trade shows and direct outbound activity.


## 1.4 Standardized Sales Process

The sales cycle follows a structured 5-stage process managed in Salesforce:
Typical sales cycle length: 30–90 days for single-rooftop franchise dealers; 60–180 days for multi-rooftop groups, depending on stakeholder complexity and incumbent contract timing.


## 1.5 Most Effective and Least Effective Sales Practices

Most Effective:
Value-anchored discovery using the Compliance Audit document — surfaces latent risk the dealer has not quantified, creating urgency without pressure
ROI-led conversations quantifying: recontract reduction, bureau cost controls via CreditGuardrails, time-per-deal savings, Desk Optimization, and cost of one fraud event
Trade show pipeline generation (NADA, regional shows) — produces high-intent prospects with compressed sales cycles due to live demos and in-person relationship building
Referral-led expansion within dealer groups — once a rooftop is live and satisfied, group expansion is faster, with lower CAC and shorter cycle, Buy/Sells are worth mention as larger groups purchase smaller dealerships or groups.
Persona-specific selling — tailoring discovery and closing language to the Dealer Principle (risk/liability), CFO (cost/margin), GSM (speed/process), or Compliance Officer (audit protection) drives significantly higher conversion
Least Effective:
Feature-first demos without prior discovery — leads to price-shopping rather than value-based decisions
Selling compliance to non-compliance-oriented personas without first establishing risk awareness — 'compliance doesn't apply to us' is a fast dead end without a pivot to fraud or cost angles
Excessive discounting at the outset — undermines the value architecture and trains customers to expect concessions on renewal

chunks.filename: Project Forge - GTM Sales Deep Dive Responses.docx
chunks.content:
## 3.3 Guardrail Strategy and Customer Messaging

Guardrails are usage controls embedded in the tier structure that optimize credit spend and protect Informativ's gross margin against over-usage that would erode COGS coverage. Each tier includes a defined number of included credit pulls, with overage usage billed at defined rates.
How Guardrails Are Messaged to Customers:
Guardrails are presented as a benefit to the dealer, not a restriction. The core message is credit spend optimization: most dealers pull 30–40% more hard pulls than necessary to fund a deal, particularly on deals that never close. Guardrails encourage a soft pull-first workflow (pre-qualify without compliance burden) and reserve hard pulls for qualified, desk-ready buyers. This reduces total credit expense while improving compliance posture.
Messaging framework: 'We help you control your credit spend — the same way you control inventory costs. The goal isn't fewer credit reports; it's smarter credit reports tied to deals that actually close.'
Handling Customer Pushback on Price Increases / Overages:
Anchor the overage conversation in bureau cost context — bureau pricing has increased industry-wide; Informativ's guardrail and soft pull tools are the most effective mitigation available
Demonstrate ROI: soft pull pre-qualification reduces hard pull volume on lost deals; the math typically shows net savings even with guardrails applied
Escalation path: customer concerns above RSM authority go to the Deal Desk; pricing exceptions require CRO approval and are evaluated against margin floor targets aligned with board guidance


## 3.4 Upsell and Cross-Sell Strategy Under New Tier Model

The new tier architecture creates a clear upsell progression with each step delivering quantifiable additional value:
Protect to Enrich: driven by SmartPencil adoption — any dealer experiencing rehash (deals reworked after initial structure) is a candidate; ROI is quantified by time saved per deal and front-end gross improvement
Enrich to Elevate: driven by credit spend management conversations — high-volume dealers with bureau cost visibility are natural candidates; soft pull strategy ROI is quantifiable
Elevate to Control: driven by lender connectivity and hard pull requirements — dealers with diverse lending relationships who need both soft and hard pull flexibility at scale
Cross-sell motion: Verified STIPs and Payments are sold as add-ons at any tier to existing Total Solution customers where funding delays or STIPs friction has been identified as a pain point


# 4. Pipeline, Conversion, and Forecasting



## 4.1 Pipeline Entry and Stage Definitions

Opportunities enter the pipeline through multiple sources managed in Salesforce. Stage definitions are standardized across the team:


## 4.2 Current Pipeline Overview

As of February 26, 2026, the active pipeline (Q2 2026, with close dates 1/1/2026–4/30/2026) was exported from Salesforce. The pipeline reflects opportunities across all stages and product solutions. Key observations:

The pipeline reflects the new tier architecture with Protect, Enrich, Elevate, and Control opportunities alongside legacy CBC/Total Solution renewals and upsells
Channel-sourced pipeline (Affiliate, CUDL, partner referrals) continues to grow as a percentage of total, reflecting the maturation of the affiliate program
A current pipeline export has been provided as a separate deliverable ('Q2 2025 Pipeline' export, as of 2/26/2026).

The following table reflects Closed Won ACV by year based on Salesforce data:
Note: The 2025 flat performance relative to 2024 reflects the period of tier architecture transition, pricing rationalization (retirement of low-margin Protect customers), unprecendented bureau price increases/overages, and the Simon-Kucher pricing engagement. The business enters 2026 with a structurally improved tier mix, higher average contract values in the new architecture, and a robust NADA-generated pipeline.

# 5. Marketing and Demand Generation



## 5.1 Sales-Marketing Alignment

Marketing and sales operate in a coordinated motion, with sales intelligence directly informing campaign strategy and content development. Key mechanisms include:
Sales rep feedback on objections, win/loss patterns, and competitive intel is incorporated into updated battle cards, objection handling playbooks, and persona-based messaging
Marketing produces the content library (1-pagers, whitepapers, case studies, solution sheets) that reps deploy throughout the sales cycle; all content is organized and maintained in a shared sales content library
NADA and trade show strategy is co-developed by sales and marketing, with messaging, booth content, and follow-up sequencing aligned pre-event
The Affiliate program is jointly managed, with marketing supporting partner enablement materials and co-branded content

## 5.2 Successful Marketing Initiatives

NADA Annual Conference Pipeline Generation:
NADA consistently produces the highest-quality pipeline of any single channel. The combination of in-person demos, curated prospect scheduling, and follow-up sequencing through Outreach compresses discovery-to-demo cycle significantly vs. cold outbound. NADA 2026 generated a strong cohort of opportunities now in active pipeline.
Total Solution Repackaging Campaign:
The unification of CBC, DSGSS, and CreditDriver into the Total Solution — positioned as 'Credit, Compliance, and Enriched Lead Data in One Platform' — was supported by a comprehensive messaging refresh including updated decks, solution sheets, case studies (Cavender Auto Family, Fremont Toyota, Kunes Auto Group), and whitepaper content. This campaign contributed directly to 30%+ bookings growth in the period following the Total Solution launch and drove Total Solution penetration to 60%+ of new sales.


## 5.3 Channel Partner Compensation

The Affiliate / Channel partner program compensates referral partners on a commission or revenue share basis. Program economics are structured to:
Reward partners for warm, qualified referrals that convert to new logo opportunities
Differentiate compensation by product — Total Solution referrals carry a higher partner payout than credit-only referrals, aligned with the company's preference to drive higher-margin TS adoption
Ensure partner economics are sustainable relative to Informativ's gross margin profile — the affiliate channel carries a lower CAC than outbound and produces high-intent leads
Specific referral fee schedules and rev share percentages can be provided under separate cover.


# 6. Packaging, Pricing, and ROI



## 6.1 Pricing Model and Contract Terms

Informativ's pricing model is structured as a hybrid subscription + usage model:
The Simon-Kucher pricing engagement (underway) is formalizing the pricing architecture across the new Protect/Enrich/Elevate/Control tier structure, with explicit attention to the margin floor by tier, guardrail design, and overage pricing mechanics. Board-aligned gross margin targets are the guiding constraint.


## 6.2 Pricing Pressures and Market Dynamics

Credit bureau price increases: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion have raised wholesale pricing, creating COGS pressure that has required selective price adjustments to defend margin. Informativ’s strategy in Enrich and Elevate tiers are the primary structural response — reducing hard pull (Elevate) or softpull (Enrich) price/volume per customer reduces the COGS impact
700Credit & NCC price competition: 700Credit & NCC consistently prices below Informativ on credit-only deals. Informativ's response is value-based positioning — demonstrating that the tiers replace 3–5 point solution vendors and delivers net ROI vs. a cheaper single-product comparison
Market sensitivity: Multi-rooftop dealer groups are sophisticated buyers who will model total cost of ownership; the ROI Calculator and cost consolidation narrative are essential tools in these conversations


## 6.3 ROI Demonstration Framework

Informativ demonstrates ROI consistently through a quantified, dealer-specific analysis. The ROI Calculator (Excel-based, available as a sales tool) models:
The ROI conversation is anchored differently depending on persona: CFOs lead with cost consolidation and bureau spend; GSMs and GMs lead with time per deal and desk efficiency; Dealer Principals / CEOs lead with fraud risk quantification and compliance liability exposure.
For Total Solution vs. Credit Standalone: TS ROI conversations are materially richer — the compliance, fraud, and SmartPencil elements add value drivers that credit-only comparisons cannot match. Credit standalone ROI is more narrowly anchored to bureau cost and soft pull savings.


# 7. Customer Journey / Account Success



## 7.1 Customer Journey: Onboarding Through Renewal

Following deal close, the customer transitions from Sales to Customer Success via a structured handoff. The journey phases are:


## 7.2 Cross-Sell and Upsell Motion

Verified STIPs: triggered when RSM/CSA/CSM identifies funding delay patterns or lender kickback activity in the account — any dealer with recontract history is a natural candidate
SmartPencil / Payments: triggered when the desk is experiencing rehash, front-end gross compression, or time-at-desk inefficiency — quantified via deal data where available
Tier upgrade (e.g., Protect to Enrich): triggered by SmartPencil adoption conversation; ROI model shows desk profitability improvement
Multi-rooftop expansion: once one location is live, group expansion is driven by demonstrating group-level compliance dashboards, centralized reporting, and economies of scale on subscription pricing


## 7.3 Churn Prevention

The most common drivers of churn and the corresponding mitigation strategies:


## 7.4 Market-Driven Upsell vs. True Upsell

Informativ distinguishes between two upsell types for revenue quality assessment:
Market-driven upsell: Revenue increases driven by bureau cost pass-throughs, usage overages (guardrail enforcement), or mandated price adjustments. These improve revenue but reflect market pricing dynamics rather than product expansion.
True upsell: Revenue increases driven by tier upgrades (e.g., Protect to Enrich), net new add-on product adoption (SmartPencil, Verified STIPs, Payments), or multi-rooftop expansion within an existing group. These reflect genuine expansion of the platform relationship and are the primary indicator of healthy net revenue retention.
Management tracks true upsell separately in Salesforce (Deal Type = 'Upsell' or 'Cross Sell' on new product/tier opportunities) to ensure expansion revenue quality is accurately represented in board and investor reporting.

## Key Competitive Insight

The three primary direct competitors identified in the SWOT assessment are: 700Credit (credit/compliance), National Credit Center (NCC, credit/compliance), and Dealertrack / Cox Automotive (F&I, compliance, digital retailing). Informativ's differentiation is sustained by platform breadth (the only unified credit + compliance + fraud + payments solution), compliance-native architecture, and white-glove service — not price. The primary competitive risk is adoption friction and price uncertainty, which the new tier structure and Simon-Kucher pricing engagement are directly designed to address.

Rules of Engagement for Informativ-ComplyAuto Partnership
Lead Definition: A lead is defined as a prospect who has been contacted and has expressed interest in learning more about the services offered by either Informativ or ComplyAuto.
Lead Attribution:
Submission Process: The referring party must document leads via a webform submission.  Each company will maintain its own webform to document referrals in its system of record.
Evaluation of Existing Opportunities: Upon receiving a lead, Revenue Operations will evaluate if there is an existing opportunity in progress for that lead:
If an opportunity exists and there is verified activity (phone conversations or meetings) within the last 30 days, the opportunity will remain with the company managing it.
Automated mass communications or marketing emails do not qualify as verified activity.
No Existing Opportunity: If no existing opportunity with verified activity exists, the lead will be attributed to the referring party in the system of record.
Each company will provide the other party with monthly reports on documented referrals and referrals received to ensure alignment.
Post-Assignment Rules: Once an account is reassigned to the referring party, it is off-limits for the internal sales team to pursue.
In cases of disputes, Management will make the final determination.
Reassignment After “Closed Lost”: If an opportunity is marked as “Closed Lost” or deemed non-viable, the prospect becomes eligible to be pursued again by either the Referring Party or the assigned Regional Sales Manager (RSM).
Commission Splits: The Company reserves the right to establish a commission split with its internal sales team to help close a deal.  Management will determine This.  The party conducting the commission split will incur any added expenses related to the commission split.
Licensing Fee Attribution Rules
The licensing fee attribution between Informativ and ComplyAuto depends on who generated the lead and completed the sale.  The following scenarios clarify fee calculations:
Scenario 1: ComplyAuto Sold Guardian First, Then Informativ
ComplyAuto License Fee: No attribution for that rooftop.
Informativ License Fee: Counts toward calculation.
Scenario 2: Neither Party Sold to the Dealer, and ComplyAuto Sells Both Products
ComplyAuto License Fee: No attribution for that rooftop.
Informativ License Fee: Counts toward calculation.
Scenario 3: Dealer is a Current Informativ Customer but Not a ComplyAuto Guardian Customer, and Informativ Sells Guardian
ComplyAuto License Fee: Counts toward the calculation.
Informativ License Fee: No attribution for that rooftop.
Scenario 4: Neither Party Sold to the Dealer, and Informativ Sells Both Products
ComplyAuto License Fee: Counts toward the calculation.
Informativ License Fee: No attribution for that rooftop.
Important Note: The party providing the lead (per the defined process) determines which entity earns the sale attribution.
Compensation Plan for Informativ Sales Team
The Informativ Sales Team will be compensated on all deals where Informativ initiates the lead, and ComplyAuto confirms that it is attributed to Informativ.
Any confirmed leads provided by ComplyAuto that are confirmed to be rightfully attributed to ComplyAuto will be excluded from consideration for commission or bonus payment.
All ComplyAuto opportunities will count towards a separate quota in addition to the assigned quota for 2025.
Sales team members will be compensated as follows:
SAAS Revenue: 5% commission on SAAS revenue for Total Solution sales.
Credit Revenue: 2.5% commission on credit revenue for Total Solution sales.
ComplyAuto Bonus: 10% of the License Fee Payment attributed to Informativ sales from ComplyAuto.
Escalation and Dispute Resolution
All disputes regarding lead attribution, opportunity assignment, or commission splits will be escalated to Management for final determination.
Both parties must maintain transparency and professional communication during the dispute-resolution process.
Collaboration Guidelines
Informativ sales team members must coordinate with ComplyAuto counterparts to ensure a seamless customer experience.
Regular check-ins and updates between the two teams should be facilitated to avoid overlaps or conflicts in lead handling.
Summary of Key Points
Leads must be submitted through a web form and evaluated for existing opportunities.
Attribution depends on verified activity within the last 30 days.
Once a lead is assigned, it is off-limits to the other party unless the opportunity becomes “Closed Lost.”
Licensing fees and commissions are determined by who generates the lead and completes the sale.
Sales team members are incentivized with competitive commission and bonus structures.

chunks.source: Informativ Payments — NADA Conference Talk Tracks
chunks.content:
Informativ Payments — NADA Conference Talk Tracks (Event Selling)

15-SECOND INTRO:
"In case you don't know, Informativ is a tech software company that provides credit and compliance software for over 5,000 dealerships across the country, and here at NADA, we are introducing our new payment product."

30-SECOND TALK TRACK:
Historically, payments meant OfferLogix or MarketScan — solid for their time, but expensive, slow, and disconnected from real credit workflows. Informativ already powers a huge part of the industry's credit and compliance infrastructure, and now we're bringing that same accuracy into payments. We deliver real, credit-qualified monthly payments in real time, at the VIN level — not estimates. These are fundable payments based on real lender programs, incentives, and dealer pricing, with profit and compliance built in. You send us a VIN and zip code, we send back an accurate payment in milliseconds. For channel partners, this is a new category: modern, faster, more accurate, and far more cost-effective than legacy payment engines.

1-MINUTE TALK TRACK:
Informativ already sits behind a huge portion of the industry's credit, compliance, and customer insight infrastructure, powering thousands of dealerships and processing millions of credit transactions every year. What's new is extending that foundation into payments. Today, most online payments are still estimated or guessed — and with affordability being the biggest issue in auto, that gap between online and in-store numbers kills trust and kills deals.

Informativ Payments delivers real, credit-qualified monthly payments, in real time, at the VIN level. These are fundable payments based on real lender programs, live incentives, and dealer pricing — with dealer profit and compliance baked in.

From a product standpoint: You send us a VIN and zip code. We return an accurate loan or lease payment in milliseconds. That means ads, marketplaces, CRMs, chatbots — every touchpoint — can show what a customer can actually afford.

For partners, this is a paradigm shift: modern APIs, usage-based pricing, faster, more accurate, and more cost-effective than legacy players — all bundled with credit and compliance.

TARGET: AI chat providers, conversational AI platforms, chatbot companies serving automotive dealers

THE PROBLEM:
With AI Chat-based platforms, the final mile of engagement is left unattended when it comes to addressing monthly payment type questions. Even LLMs like OpenAI and Anthropic break when questions around vehicle monthly payments are surfaced in chat instances. The AI can't answer the #1 consumer question: "What's my real payment?"

PITCH POSITIONING:
Informativ's payment APIs are built to be easily ingested into AI chat products. Partners can return credit-qualified payments in-chat via soft pull, boosting engagement and delivering higher-intent, payment-ready leads. The API returns real, fundable monthly payments in milliseconds — turning the AI assistant from an information tool into a transaction-ready experience.

KEY DIFFERENTIATORS FOR AI PARTNERS:
- Solves the "final mile" problem in AI chat — real payment answers, not estimates
- REST APIs designed for easy integration into conversational AI workflows
- Soft credit pull — no impact to consumer credit score
- Returns loan and lease scenarios in milliseconds
- Credit-qualified, VIN-specific, and locally accurate (taxes, fees, incentives)
- Usage-based pricing aligned with AI platform economics

Informativ Payments — Global Ad Messaging and LinkedIn DM Templates

UNIVERSAL VALUE PROP (ALL USE CASES):
No matter where automotive shoppers start their journey — digital ads, dealer websites, digital retailing, or chat conversations — the question they care about most is simple: "What will my actual monthly payment be?" Today, most platforms still show generic or estimated numbers. That gap costs engagement, trust, and conversions.

Informativ Payments solves this for any automotive platform by returning real, credit-qualified, VIN-specific monthly payments using a soft pull with no impact to consumer credit.

Partners use Informativ's Payments API because it: Builds trust with accurate, fundable payments. Improves shopper engagement with real numbers that keep people in the flow. Delivers higher-intent leads with true affordability context. Requires no workflow changes — simply plug Informativ's Payments API into your existing experience. Scales economically with usage-based pricing — pay for only the VINs you want payments for.

HEADLINE: "Payments API for Auto Software"

LINKEDIN DM TEMPLATES BY USE CASE:

chunks.source: Credit 101 - Unlock the Power of Credit Reports & Scores For Your Business With Informativ
chunks.filename: Credit 101 - Unlock the Power of Credit Reports & Scores For Your Business With Informativ.pdf
chunks.content:
HARD VS. SOFT PULLS: THE CREDIT INQUIRY DIFFERENCE
Hard Pulls: Comprehensive but Impactful
What They Are: A hard pull is a more invasive credit check, typically triggered by a credit
application for a major financial product, like a car loan.
Impact: These inquiries can cause a temporary dip in the consumer’s credit score.
Compliance: Hard pulls require more extensive regulatory obligations, including consumer
consent, documentation of permissible purpose, and timely disclosures like adverse action notices
and risk-based pricing or credit score exception notices.
Soft Pulls: The Superior Approach
Soft pulls are gaining significant ground in lending because they deliver the same critical information
as hard pulls, but with one massive advantage—they don’t affect the consumer’s credit score. This
makes them a powerful, non-invasive tool for businesses looking to make informed lending decisions
while protecting their customers’ credit health.
What They Are: A soft pull is a lighter inquiry that doesn’t impact a consumer’s credit score. It’s
ideal for prequalification or marketing offers.
Why Soft Pulls Are Superior:
No Credit Score Impact: The biggest advantage of soft pulls is that they allow lenders to assess a
borrower’s financial profile without lowering their credit score. This is a win-win for both parties—
lenders get the insights they need, and consumers don’t suffer any negative impact.
Same Detailed Information: Soft pulls can offer the same level of detail as hard pulls, down to an
identical credit score, giving you all the data you need to make informed decisions.
Compliance Advantage: Soft pulls come with fewer regulatory
burdens. Generally, there’s no need for adverse action notices, or risk-
based pricing or credit score exception notices, making them easier
and faster to use in early-stage credit assessments.
Tailored Insights: Beyond the credit score, soft pulls provide a
snapshot of a borrower’s payment history, debt obligations, and
credit behavior. These insights allow businesses to tailor offers or
prequalification notices without any impact to the consumer’s credit.
In summary, soft pulls are a superior method for businesses that want to
protect their customers’ credit scores while still accessing all the necessary
credit related data. This makes them perfect for prequalification, early-stage
assessments, and marketing offers.

Finding the Right Balance for Your Business
Credit reports are essential in lending decisions, but so are credit scores. As you refine your approach,
consider which tools—soft or hard pulls, single or multiple reports—best align with your strategy. Soft pulls
should be at the core of your lending process, offering a seamless, risk-free way to gather information while
not impacting your customers’ credit scores.
Mastering the use of soft pulls, alongside a keen understanding of credit reports and scores, will empower
your business to manage risk more effectively, maintain compliance, and enhance customer relationships.
WANT TO LEARN MORE ABOUT INFORMATIV’S CREDIT,
COMPLIANCE & CUSTOMER INSIGHTS SOLUTIONS?
Contact us today for a free 10-minute demo at
hello@informativ.com.
800.448.0183 hello@informativ.com informativ.com

chunks.filename: Informativ Enhances Your Business & Empowers Your Lending Decisions with Alternative Credit Data.pdf
chunks.content:
LOOKING AHEAD: THE FUTURE OF ALTERNATIVE CREDIT DATA
The role of alternative credit data is expanding, with key trends shaping the future of
financial decision-making:
Deeper Integration with Financial Technology – AI-driven analysis of non-traditional
financial data is enhancing real-time decision-making.
Broader Industry Adoption – More industries, including insurance and real estate, are
incorporating alternative credit data into their assessments.
A Stronger Push for Financial Inclusion – Efforts to bridge the credit gap are growing,
helping millions of underserved consumers access financial products.
Financial institutions that embrace these advancements will be better positioned to serve a
diverse customer base while mitigating lending risks.
As the financial landscape continues to evolve, and as consumers and the work
environment changes, adopting alternative credit data is no longer just an option—it’s a
necessity. Businesses must unlock new credit opportunities while maintaining a strong,
risk-aware lending strategy.
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (FAQS)
How does rent payment credit reporting impact creditworthiness assessment?
Many consumers don’t realize that rent payments can contribute to their credit history. Rent
payment data, when reported to credit bureaus, can provide a valuable indicator of financial
responsibility. Lenders who incorporate rental payment history into their models can extend
credit opportunities to individuals with limited traditional credit history. Rent payment history
is not typically included in traditional credit reports but is a vital component of alternative
credit data reports
What role does bank transaction data play in expanding credit access?
Bank transaction data provides a real-time view of an individual’s income stability and spending
habits. By analyzing cash flow patterns, lenders can better assess financial behavior and extend
credit to responsible borrowers who might otherwise be overlooked due to a lack of traditional
credit history.
How does alternative credit data enhance fraud prevention?
By analyzing non-traditional financial behaviors, such as irregular payment patterns or inconsistent
financial activity or credit usage changes or inquiry velocity, lenders can more effectively identify
fraudulent applications. Alternative data sources provide deeper insights, helping financial
institutions strengthen fraud detection without compromising fair lending practices.
SEE HOW INFORMATIV’S SOLUTIONS WILL ENHANCE YOUR
BUSINESS & EMPOWER YOUR LENDING DECISIONS.
CONTACT US TODAY TO LEARN MORE!
800.448.0183 hello@informativ.com informativ.com
